works.title: A Paleogenomic Reconstruction of the Deep Population History of the Andes
works.authors: Nathan Nakatsuka; Iosif Lazaridis; Chiara Barbieri; Pontus Skoglund; Nadin Rohland; Swapan Mallick; Cosimo Posth; Kelly Harkins-Kinkaid; Matthew Ferry; Éadaoin Harney; Megan Michel; Kristin Stewardson; Jannine Forst; José M. Capriles; Marta Alfonso Durruty; Karina Aranda Álvarez; David Beresford‐Jones; Richard L. Burger; Lauren Cadwallader; Ricardo Fujita; Johny Isla; George Lau; Carlos Lémuz Aguirre; Steven A. LeBlanc; Sergio Calla Maldonado; Frank Meddens; Pablo G. Messineo; Brendan J. Culleton; Thomas K. Harper; Jeffrey Quilter; Gustavo Politis; Kurt Rademaker; Markus Reindel; Mario A. Rivera; Lucy C. Salazar; José R. Sandoval; Calógero M. Santoro; Nahuel A. Scheifler; Vivien G. Standen; Maria Inês Barreto; Isabel Flores Espinoza; Elsa Tomasto-Cagigao; Guido Valverde; Douglas J. Kennett; Alan Cooper; Johannes Krause; Wolfgang Haak; Bastien Llamas; David Reich; Lars Fehren‐Schmitz
works.abstract: There are many unanswered questions about the population history of the Central and South Central Andes, particularly regarding the impact of large-scale societies, such as the Moche, Wari, Tiwanaku, and Inca. We assembled genome-wide data on 89 individuals dating from ∼9,000-500 years ago (BP), with a particular focus on the period of the rise and fall of state societies. Today's genetic structure began to develop by 5,800 BP, followed by bi-directional gene flow between the North and South Highlands, and between the Highlands and Coast. We detect minimal admixture among neighboring groups between ∼2,000-500 BP, although we do detect cosmopolitanism (people of diverse ancestries living side-by-side) in the heartlands of the Tiwanaku and Inca polities. We also highlight cases of long-range mobility connecting the Andes to Argentina and the Northwest Andes to the Amazon Basin. VIDEO ABSTRACT.

works.title: On the Use of Biomineral Oxygen Isotope Data to Identify Human Migrants in the Archaeological Record: Intra-Sample Variation, Statistical Methods and Geographical Considerations
works.abstract: Oxygen isotope analysis of archaeological skeletal remains is an increasingly popular tool to study past human migrations. It is based on the assumption that human body chemistry preserves the δ18O of precipitation in such a way as to be a useful technique for identifying migrants and, potentially, their homelands. In this study, the first such global survey, we draw on published human tooth enamel and bone bioapatite data to explore the validity of using oxygen isotope analyses to identify migrants in the archaeological record. We use human δ18O results to show that there are large variations in human oxygen isotope values within a population sample. This may relate to physiological factors influencing the preservation of the primary isotope signal, or due to human activities (such as brewing, boiling, stewing, differential access to water sources and so on) causing variation in ingested water and food isotope values. We compare the number of outliers identified using various statistical methods. We determine that the most appropriate method for identifying migrants is dependent on the data but is likely to be the IQR or median absolute deviation from the median under most archaeological circumstances. Finally, through a spatial assessment of the dataset, we show that the degree of overlap in human isotope values from different locations across Europe is such that identifying individuals' homelands on the basis of oxygen isotope analysis alone is not possible for the regions analysed to date. Oxygen isotope analysis is a valid method for identifying first-generation migrants from an archaeological site when used appropriately, however it is difficult to identify migrants using statistical methods for a sample size of less than c. 25 individuals. In the absence of local previous analyses, each sample should be treated as an individual dataset and statistical techniques can be used to identify migrants, but in most cases pinpointing a specific homeland should not be attempted.

works.title: Google Earth as a Powerful Tool for Archaeological and Cultural Heritage Applications: A Review
works.authors: Lei Luo; Xinyuan Wang; Huadong Guo; Rosa Lasaponara; Pilong Shi; Nabil Bachagha; Li Li; Ya Yao; Nicola Masini; Fulong Chen; Wei Ji; Hui Cao; Chao Li; Ningke Hu
works.abstract: Google Earth (GE), a large Earth-observation data-based geographical information computer application, is an intuitive three-dimensional virtual globe. It enables archaeologists around the world to communicate and share their multisource data and research findings. Different from traditional geographical information systems (GIS), GE is free and easy to use in data collection, exploration, and visualization. In the past decade, many peer-reviewed articles on the use of GE in the archaeological cultural heritage (ACH) research field have been published. Most of these concern specific ACH investigations with a wide spatial coverage. GE can often be used to survey and document ACH so that both skilled archaeologists and the public can more easily and intuitively understand the results. Based on geographical tools and multi-temporal very high-resolution (VHR) satellite imagery, GE has been shown to provide spatio-temporal change information that has a bearing on the physical, environmental, and geographical character of ACH. In this review, in order to discuss the huge potential of GE, a comprehensive review of GE and its applications to ACH in the published scientific literature is first presented; case studies in five main research fields demonstrating how GE can be deployed as a key tool for studying ACH are then described. The selected case studies illustrate how GE can be used effectively to investigate ACH at multiple scales, discover new archaeological sites in remote regions, monitor historical sites, and assess damage in areas of conflict, and promote virtual tourism. These examples form the basis for highlighting current trends in remote sensing archaeology based on the GE platform, which could provide access to a low-cost and easy-to-use tool for communicating and sharing ACH geospatial data more effectively to the general public in the era of Digital Earth. Finally, a discussion of the merits and limitations of GE is presented along with conclusions and remaining challenges.

works.title: Reconstructing the Population Genetic History of the Caribbean
works.authors: Andrés Moreno‐Estrada; Simon Gravel; Fouad Zakharia; Jacob L. McCauley; Jake Byrnes; Christopher R. Gignoux; Patricia A. Ortiz-Tello; Ricardo Martínez; Dale J. Hedges; Richard Morris; Celeste Eng; Karla Sandoval; Suehelay Acevedo‐Acevedo; Paul J. Norman; Z. Layrisse; Peter Parham; Juan Carlos Martínez‐Cruzado; Esteban G. Burchard; Michael L. Cuccaro; Eden R. Martin; Carlos D. Bustamante
works.abstract: The Caribbean basin is home to some of the most complex interactions in recent history among previously diverged human populations. Here, we investigate the population genetic history of this region by characterizing patterns of genome-wide variation among 330 individuals from three of the Greater Antilles (Cuba, Puerto Rico, Hispaniola), two mainland (Honduras, Colombia), and three Native South American (Yukpa, Bari, and Warao) populations. We combine these data with a unique database of genomic variation in over 3,000 individuals from diverse European, African, and Native American populations. We use local ancestry inference and tract length distributions to test different demographic scenarios for the pre-and postcolonial history of the region. We develop a novel ancestry-specific PCA (ASPCA) method to reconstruct the sub-continental origin of Native American, European, and African haplotypes from admixed genomes. We find that the most likely source of the indigenous ancestry in Caribbean islanders is a Native South American component shared among inland Amazonian tribes, Central America, and the Yucatan peninsula, suggesting extensive gene flow across the Caribbean in pre-Columbian times. We find evidence of two pulses of African migration. The first pulse-which today is reflected by shorter, older ancestry tracts-consists of a genetic component more similar to coastal West African regions involved in early stages of the trans-Atlantic slave trade. The second pulse-reflected by longer, younger tracts-is more similar to present-day West-Central African populations, supporting historical records of later transatlantic deportation. Surprisingly, we also identify a Latino-specific European component that has significantly diverged from its parental Iberian source populations, presumably as a result of small European founder population size. We demonstrate that the ancestral components in admixed genomes can be traced back to distinct sub-continental source populations with far greater resolution than previously thought, even when limited pre-Columbian Caribbean haplotypes have survived.

works.title: Earthworks Rising: Mound Building in Native Literature and Arts
works.abstract: A few years ago, before the pandemic, I was invited to visit a group of geoglyphs in the Sonoran Desert called the Blythe Intaglios, outside Blythe, California, along the Colorado River separating California from Arizona. These massive glyphs are scraped into the same desert whose hostility the US Border Patrol has recruited in its cynical policy of “prevention through deterrence,” creating what anthropologist Jason de León (2015) calls “the land of open graves,” insofar as border police enlist the heat, hostility, and danger of the desert to do the dirty work of migrant deterrence. This realization came to me as I was introduced to the glyphs by their local caretaker, who insisted, on the contrary, that they were signals of life, of the creation of life. They marked the location of the primordial home of the Mexica, Aztlán, whose cosmogonic caves—the caves of Chicomoztoc—were nearby in the Big Maria Mountains. This caretaker is named Alfredo Figueroa, a lively octogenarian organizer, activist, and historian, a veteran of the Chicano branch of the civil rights movement, who had more recently dedicated himself to the preservation and understanding of these glyphs. He was their defender—having stood up for them in the face of damage from inconsiderate trekkers and overeager solar farm corporations—but, more provocatively, he was also their storyteller. Through the glyphs, he unfolded accounts from the creation of all humanity (not just the Mexica) to his own inspiration in the face of racial violence in Blythe and surrounding towns. In my experience of the intaglios with him, they were features of a poetry of the landscape and, though certainly historical, not only so.That said, archeological questions arise: how old are the glyphs and who made them? Radiocarbon dating suggests the Blythe glyphs were made in waves from a temporal maximum of 900 BC to a minimum of 550–1150 AD (von Werholf et al. 1995: 268). And, though the exact names of the makers of the glyphs are lost to time, their connection to local indigenous tribes is not. Nearby Chemehuevi, Hopi, Mohave, and Diné people maintain relation with them. What are they exactly? They are a group of massive (the largest is 171 feet—about the size of a sequoia tree) human, animal, and spirit figures scraped into the desert surface, not unlike the Nazca Lines of Peru. They also appear to have archaeoastronomical features—that is, elements that suggest use as time-space coordinates by way of the sun, moon, and stars in the sky. These are all facts, and important ones; yet even as I write about them here, I struggle with their telling because such accounts don't seem to me to engage properly with the glyphs—with my experience of them alongside Figueroa and his family. For some time, I discussed the glyphs in academic talks with what came to feel like a frustrating tinge of archeological presumption. And it was only until I stopped talking about the glyphs in the framework of a talk, and started talking about them in story, that I felt released from that intellectual centrifuge that wishes to completely capture the tangibility of an intellectual object. But this raised many questions for me: Why stories? That is, what kind of holding and passing along does a story do that a talk does not, especially with respect to such earthworks? What, in other words, is the value of the literary in those scenes of terrestrial investigation that have hitherto been given over solely to archaeology? And, building on that question, what is gained in telling a story—divergent, layered, fuzzy, even in touch with the intangible—that feels appropriate to the ongoing life of the Blythe Intaglios?Chadwick Allen's new book, Earthworks Rising: Mound Building in Native Literature and Arts, has brought these questions into focus for me and given them a sense of purpose. Its title indicates the book's forceful argument: earthworks and mounds are not just repositories of artifacts, bones, and radiocarbon data; they are literature and art, styles of transmission that persist as a present force of alignment and orientation. They are not mere resources for archaeological knowledge. They are flashpoints in ongoing historical and interpersonal reckoning; or, more basically, they are scenes of speaking and making, poems of the curled earth that prompt more poems. Hence, they require a poetics of the kind that Allen gives them in five multitiered chapters that are interlocked with several essayistic (i.e., autological) codas.And the book's biggest intervention is one that shows why and how a literary approach—discussed throughout the book and enacted in its codas—is proper to a study of earthworks. In this intervention is a critique of unreconstructed approaches to earthworks that have rendered them nothing but inert platforms for historical self-mystification at best and at worst symptoms of a pathological compulsion to dig for historical redemption that cannot be found in digging. The book engages a stunning range of objects: the mathematically and geometrically exquisite poetry of Allison Hedge Coke, the complex racial and geospatial locations of Jimmie Durham's sculptural installations, the referentially deft multimedia collages of Alyssa Hinton, the interlinguistic contact points of Margaret Noodin's poems, and the intellectual inheritance of the architectural design of the First Americans Museum in Oklahoma City, as well the extensive historical fiction and fictional historicism around the mounds, not to mention the mounds themselves, especially in Illinois and Ohio. As we have come to expect from Allen's books (Blood Narrative in 2002 and Trans-Indigenous in 2012), the close readings are exquisite, with an attention to detail whose labor always scales. His readings give sight of disciplinary perspectives that complicate facile conceptions of poetry, literature, indigeneity, colonialism, historiography, and research itself. The conventions of a book review prevent me from giving extensive account of the many intellectual sightlines in Allen's book. Instead, I will focus on one that seems to me overarching, that clarifies long-standing problems for me, indeed that troubles and helps me to reflect on my own disciplinary formation. I mean the question: How does literary studies, and even poetry as such, encounter the mounds of the Americas? Furthermore, how does that literary encounter create a particular or even special relation to the mounds?In his reading of Hinton's digital collage “Ancestral Plane” (1998), Allen engages with an image (featured on the book's cover), conveying its historical density without giving over to an archaeological mode of excavating, disinterring, or raiding. As with many of his readings, he sets the stage with a wide array of related objects and historical episodes—in this case, the most dramatic is a drawing made by nineteenth-century Irish American artist John J. Egan for amateur archaeologist and mounds enthusiast Montroville Wilson Dickinson. Egan's images are breathtaking for both their historical literalness and disciplinary symbolism. One, titled “Huge Mound and the Manner of Opening Them,” shows two white archeologists directing a group of enslaved black laborers (the scene takes place on a plantation in 1843 Louisiana) to dig up a mound whose treasures (human remains, clay urns, and various objects) are shown in cross-section while, to the far right, a group of living indigenous people seem uninterested in the scene (but not enough to be excluded from the picture), sort of on their way out. The picture or “complex vignette” sets up the temporality of archaeology: clearly differentiating between who is dead (the mysterious mounds builders), who departs (the unconcerned Indians), who should work (the black laborers), and who directs the whole operation (the white scientists) (255). With this intellectual “stratigraphy” (25), to crib Allen's terms, he describes the troubled warrant of archeology in its relationally racial distinction between the admirable past of the Americas and its less admirable (hence removable) present indigenous inhabitants, let alone the absolutely unadmirable labor force exported from Africa for the work, here scientific but also quite spiritual, of connecting white intellect to American earth. Identifying a “visual empathy” between Egan's drawing and Hinton's collage, he points out a peculiar new feature of thermography that she adds to the scene, as if to suggest that the problem is not only digging but also the easy scientistic availability of such sites with technologies like LiDAR—in other words, the presumption of scientific capture. Yet he notes that in her thermal cast there is a deep blue childlike body pushing up through the stratigraphic layer, as if preparing to be born out of this womblike bulge of land. Rather than “‘red’ and ‘dead,’” the radiance of blue connotes a power of creation and a sense of motion through time—“that is, creative movement that crosses realms, including between the material and the spiritual, the living and the dead” (253). This idea that the mounds and other earthworks of the Americas are not sites of seductively interred artifactual knowledge but locations of transit, transformation, and creation is carried throughout the book. With many rich readings Allen shows that these mounds are places of literary transport, artistic transformation, and actual, living movement.Hence, his reading of Hinton's collage quickly interlocks with a reading of poems by Hedge Coke. But what moved me even more than that move, important as it is, was Allen's expanded sense of what is involved with literary inquiry. For Allen and the indigenous artists and writers he writes about, poetry is not just type on a page; it is the dynamic movement of meaning over the land, transiting through conversations and the steps of feet across an intellectually present and responsive earth. Such points of contact are not external to the work of poetry. Like Hinton's blue child pushing up through so many layers, they are “networked systems . . . portals between worlds . . . conduits linking generations” (283). Thus, there was a sense that what was most powerful about the mounds in their literary conception was not what is tangible about them, but rather the opposite—that is, what is intangible: their performance, improvisation, remote communication, and even atmospheric resonance—all those things that are hard to discuss in terms of literary analysis as we tend to think about it. This emphasis is clearest when Allen discusses his own “intense dreaming,” with trepidation to be sure, but with a kind of necessary forthrightness (236, 282). While a Freudian conception of the dream would only lead us back to an archaeological sensibility, a disciplinary formation based on an idea of neuroses needing to be disinterred, Allen's conception of the dream is different. Recognizing that such “indigenous epistemologies and indigenous ways of knowing [risk] being accused of lacking intellectual rigor,” his emphasis on the very problems of disciplinary formation sets up his dreamwork in a strategic and compelling way. He helps me to see the dream as a surface—a plane, in Hinton's words—over which relations, orientations, and collaborations (transhistorical and otherwise) are made and negotiated. There never seems to be anything hidden in a dream for Allen; rather, like a story or a mound, it strives to align historical complexities and movements.This dreamwork is where I felt most brought back to my visit with the Blythe Intaglios. First, because Allen's book inspires the confidence to write, here, that I think story is the proper way to both understand my experience with the glyph caretaker as well to represent my experience with the Blythe Geoglyphs, to think, feel, and speak with them and perform them, as I am trying to do in this review. As I have suggested, Allen models this in his book by way of the essayistic codas and the various moments in which literary inquiry gives way to accounts of walking, talking, making, and dreaming. That alone will stand as a tremendous intellectual gift to scholars and writers—both helping them to ask the questions necessary for such inquiry and giving some models by which to flesh out those questions in poetry, prose, and art. But, even more personally, his book also helped me to recast aspects of my visit to the Blythe Intaglios that I had siloed to a realm of private ephemera. That is, on the night before I went out to visit the glyphs I stayed in a rented cabin on the other side of the Colorado River. Having arrived late at night, I did not see (as I would early the next morning) that the cabin grounds sat across the road from a “pioneer cemetery,” as a posted sign told. That first night, I had an intense dream of a massive crowd of dead people outside my cabin pushing their way in. Not ghosts—they didn't have that half-life—and not hostile either. They were just life from the past palpable in the present moment, variously white- and brown-skinned, in all kinds of historical and contemporary clothing. In retrospect, I may have already had in my mind my location in the “land of open graves,” colonial and contemporary. But it wasn't a sense of death, either, with which these visitors came. They were, in a sense, the past present in anticipation of my coming visit to the desert glyphs—my own witnessing of a scene of creation—a story opening up. Still, even with Allen's book to guide me, I am anxious writing this here, fearing it will be seen to lack rigor and substance. But that is exactly the point—the intangibility of that instance and the necessary confidence to tell it—of which I was reminded as Allen's powerful and necessary book so often reminds that the past is never behind you or buried beneath you; rather, as the mounds teach, it is in front of you and around you, even now, crowding the space with its needs, desires, motivations, influences, horizons, alignments, and orientations.
